answer:
We test for invalidity by trying to make all premises true and the conclusion false.  
If the conclusion \(L \supset K\) is false, then \(L\) is true and \(K\) is false.  
With \(K\) false, premise \(N \lor K\) forces \(N\) true; then premise \(N \supset \sim M\) forces \(M\) false.  
Now premise \((K \cdot L) \supset M\) has \(K\) false, so \(K \cdot L\) is false, making the conditional true regardless of \(M\).  
All premises are true with \(L\) true, \(K\) false, \(N\) true, \(M\) false, which matches option D.  

\boxed{D}
